The Water Quality Control Commission revised the following section in this rulemaking hearing: Section 
11.2(6) - Materials Incorporated by Reference. The provisions of the Colorado Revised Statutes (CRS), 
sections 25-1.5-202, and 25-8-202(1), CRS, provide specific statutory authority for adoption of these 
regulatory amendments. The Commission also adopted, in compliance with section 24-4-103(4), CRS, 
the following statement of basis and purpose. 
BASIS AND PURPOSE 
The Commission recently adopted the Lead and Copper Rule Revisions as required to retain Colorado’s 
primacy under the Safe Drinking Water Act. Those revisions incorporate by reference a number of federal 
regulations. Pursuant to 24-4-103(12.5), CRS, incorporations by reference must identify the version of 
regulations adopted by including a date. This rulemaking amends the incorporation by reference date in 
Regulation 11.2(6) from April 8, 2018 to November 13, 2023 in order to include recent changes to the 
federal regulations relevant to the Lead and Copper Rule Revisions.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1002-11 
Water Quality Control Commission 
 
441 
11.64 
STATEMENT OF BASIS, SPECIFIC STATUTORY AUTHORITY AND PURPOSE: August 11, 
2025 rulemaking; Final Action August 11, 2025; Effective Date October 15, 2025 
The Water Quality Control Commission made the following revisions in this rulemaking hearing: Adoption 
of Section 11.15 – Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule with amendments to Sections 11.2(6) - 
Materials Incorporated by Reference, 11.3 - Definitions, Acronyms and Abbreviations, 11.21(1) - Organic 
Chemicals Rule - Applicability and Definitions, 11.21(2)(b) - MCL Requirements for SOCs, 11.27(2) - 
Compositing Samples for Nitrate, Nitrite, Inorganic Chemicals, VOCs, and SOCs, 11.33 - Public 
Notification Rule, 11.34 - Consumer Confidence Report (CCR) Rule, 11.45(9) - MCLs and MCLGs for 
Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS), and 11.46(10), (11), (12) and (13) - Analytical Requirements 
and Laboratory Certification Rule. The provisions of the Colorado Revised Statutes (CRS), sections 25-
1.5-202 and 203, provide specific statutory authority for adoption of these regulatory amendments. The 
Commission also adopted, in compliance with the Colorado Administrative Procedure Act (APA), section 
24-4-103(4), CRS, the following statement of basis and purpose. 
BASIS AND PURPOSE 
Background 
All suppliers of drinking water in Colorado are subject to regulations adopted by the U.S. Environmental 
Protection Agency (EPA) under the Safe Drinking Water Act, (42 U.S.C. 300f et seq.) as well as 
regulations adopted by the Commission. EPA has granted Colorado, with the Colorado Department of 
Public Health and Environment (Department) as the administering agency, primary enforcement authority 
(primacy) for the public water system supervision program under the federal Safe Drinking Water Act. The 
Water Quality Control Division (Division) is part of the Department and is responsible for implementing 
and enforcing the drinking water regulations that are adopted by the Commission. In order to maintain 
primacy from the EPA, states must also adopt new federal regulations that are no less stringent than 
those promulgated by the federal government. In this rulemaking the Commission is adopting the Per- 
and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule and the Consumer Confidence Report Rule Revisions which are no 
less stringent than the federally-mandated PFAS Rule and CCR Rule Revisions. By retaining primacy, the 
Department is able to protect public health by ensuring that public water systems provide safe drinking 
water to Colorado citizens and visitors. 
In addition to adopting the federally-mandated Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule and Consumer 
Confidence Report Rule Revisions to maintain primacy, this rulemaking also included minor revisions to 
update outdated references, standardize formatting for consistency, and correct minor discrepancies 
between federal language in other sections. The Commission adopted these revisions in part to ensure 
requirements are no less stringent than federal rules. 
Policies, Handbooks and Guidance and Regulation 11 
The Commission adopts regulations that create binding norms or legal obligations of the Department or 
regulated entities. The Department may develop implementation policies and guidance/handbooks where 
implementation of Regulation 11 may require interpretation, decision-making flexibility, or a streamlined 
approach for meeting compliance requirements. These amendments to Regulation 11 are supported by 
guidance/handbooks that the Department intends to continue to develop as needed as part of the 
ongoing implementation of Regulation 11. 
The Division originally issued WQCD Policy Number 1, Implementation Policy Framework (Policy 1) in 
November 2010 and the associated Procedure 1 in August 2012; the Division prepared both in 
accordance with the APA.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1002-11 
Water Quality Control Commission 
 
442 
Policy 1 specifically states that implementation policies and associated procedures are not binding 
regulations and are not to be applied as such. The referenced guidance/handbooks in these amendments 
are not independent requirements. Violations or other notices of non-compliance cannot be issued 
against a policy or guidance/handbook. Violations or other notices of non-compliance can, and will, only 
be issued for a failure to comply with Regulation 11 or an applicable statute (law) included in the CRS.  
Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule  
The Commission adopted the Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule in Section 11.15. The Per- and 
Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule aims to protect public health by limiting exposure to per- and 
polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in drinking water by establishing the first-ever, national, legally-
enforceable drinking water standards for PFAS. The Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule includes 
the following provisions of the federal regulations as published in the Federal Register, Volume 89, 
Number 82, April 26, 2024, pages 32,532 through 35,757, PFAS National Primary Drinking Water 
Regulation: 
• 
Implementation of initial monitoring requirements for public water systems to detect regulated 
PFAS. 
• 
Ongoing monitoring to ensure consistent compliance with the established Maximum Contaminant 
Levels (MCLs). 
• 
Establishment of legally enforceable MCLs and Maximum Contaminant Level Goals for six 
specified PFAS and certain mixtures. 
• 
Creation of a Hazard Index to address and regulate the combined health risks of specific PFAS 
mixtures. 
• 
Requirement for public water systems to issue public notifications when PFAS levels surpass the 
established MCLs. 
• 
Mandating the implementation of approved treatment technologies to effectively reduce PFAS 
concentrations in drinking water. 
• 
Implementation of data collection and reporting requirements for public water systems to track 
and share monitoring results. 
The amendments adopted by the Commission remain as stringent as the federal requirements for the 
Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule. By adopting these amendments, the Commission is ensuring 
the Division can maintain primacy and enforcement authority for the Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances 
Rule, as published in the Federal Register, Volume 89, Number 82, April 26, 2024, pages 32,532 through 
35,757, PFAS National Primary Drinking Water Regulation, when the rule becomes. This decision reflects 
the Commission's commitment to protecting public health and safeguarding drinking water quality. 
Maintaining primacy is crucial, as it allows the state to effectively implement and enforce the federal PFAS 
requirements and other drinking water regulations, while ensuring timely and tailored responses to local 
conditions and needs. Without primacy, the state would relinquish its authority to the EPA, potentially 
leading to delays, less effective implementation of these critical safeguards, and lack of clarity and 
organization of the federal requirements. This decision also reflects discussions with stakeholders during 
the stakeholder engagement and rule drafting process which indicated a strong preference for the 
Division to propose this rule to the Commission.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1002-11 
Water Quality Control Commission 
 
443 
A complicating factor is that the EPA has announced preliminary plans to further revise the PFAS 
National Primary Drinking Water Regulation to provide greater regulatory flexibility, with a proposed rule 
likely to be issued in fall 2025 and a final revised rule likely to be issued in spring 2026. After EPA finally 
promulgates revisions to the PFAS National Primary Drinking Water Regulation, the Division commits to 
conduct a stakeholder engagement process to solicit input and further evaluate implementation 
challenges, and to return to the Commission with modifications to these amendments to align with EPA’s 
revised PFAS Rule. Indeed, the Division is obligated by law to adopt any rule revisions finalized by EPA 
to maintain primacy, as specified in 40 C.F.R. 142.12(a). To account for uncertainties due to litigation 
against the EPA and potential rule review and revisions by the new administration, the Commission 
adopted language for an automatic stay, extension, or withdrawal of implementation of any portions of the 
Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule and related requirements in the event of any future action 
related to the rule at the federal level. The Division is committed to working closely with the EPA, other 
states, and stakeholders to address these uncertainties and complexities and transition to the revised rule 
requirements as efficiently as possible. The Division will keep water systems and stakeholders informed 
about federal rulemaking actions to the fullest extent possible. 
The Commission recognizes the pervasive threat posed by PFAS contamination and the urgent need for 
proactive measures. By adopting these amendments, the state is demonstrating its dedication to staying 
ahead of federal timelines and ensuring that its regulatory framework is robust and responsive. This 
action underscores the Commission's resolve to address emerging contaminants and uphold the highest 
standards for drinking water safety. The Commission's proactive approach will allow for a more efficient 
and effective implementation of the new federal standards, which will, in turn, provide better protection for 
consumers. 
The Division engaged with stakeholders in six (6) virtual meetings between August 2024 and January 
2025 to incorporate the Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule into proposed regulatory language for 
Regulation 11. The Division and stakeholders were able to find overall consensus in bringing clarity and 
organization to the federal requirements while remaining as stringent as the federal rule.  
The Commission has adopted a recommendation made by the Division and agreed upon by the majority 
of stakeholders to modify the Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule's initial monitoring compliance 
date, moving it from April 26, 2027, to April 1, 2027, and the MCL compliance date, moving it from April 
26, 2029 to April 1, 2029. This adjustment was made to align the start date of routine monitoring with the 
beginning of a calendar quarter. The proposal seeks to improve monitoring expectations when routine 
monitoring begins and creates a more efficient and organized implementation process with existing data 
management resources. 
The Commission acknowledges that the Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Rule presents significant 
challenges for public water systems, particularly in terms of the substantial financial investments required 
for monitoring and treatment technologies. Recognizing the complexity of PFAS remediation, the 
Commission expects the Division to provide technical assistance and explore funding opportunities to 
support water systems in achieving compliance within its resource constraints. Furthermore, the 
Commission understands that smaller systems and those in vulnerable communities may face 
disproportionate burdens, and expects the Division to prioritize resources to ensure equitable 
implementation of the rule, within funding constraints.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1002-11 
Water Quality Control Commission 
 
444 
Revisions to Consumer Confidence Report Rule 
The Commission adopted the Consumer Confidence Report Rule Revisions into existing Section 11.34. 
The Consumer Confidence Report Rule Revisions aim to enhance public understanding of drinking water 
quality. These revisions mandate updates to the annual Consumer Confidence Reports, ensuring they 
provide clear and comprehensive information to consumers. The changes focus on improving the clarity 
and accessibility of water quality data, ensuring that consumers can easily understand the results of 
required monitoring and testing. The Consumer Confidence Report Rule Revisions include the following 
provisions of the federal regulations as published in the Federal Register, Volume 89, Number 102, May 
24, 2024, pages 45,980 through 46,014, National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Consumer 
Confidence Reports: 
• 
Implementation of standardized language and formatting to improve consumer comprehension of 
water quality reports, including requiring that all reports contain a summary of the most important 
information at the beginning of the report. 
• 
Establishment of clear guidelines for conveying potential health risks associated with detected 
contaminants. 
• 
Implementation of expanded electronic delivery options and revised good faith delivery options for 
consumer convenience. 
• 
Requirement for community water systems serving populations of 10,000 or more to provide 
Consumer Confidence Reports biannually. 
• 
Mandating that community water systems serving populations of 100,000 or more provide 
translation services or translated reports in languages spoken by a significant portion of their 
service area. 
Through three (3) virtual meetings and one (1) hybrid meeting held between September 2024 and 
January 2025, the Division and stakeholders worked together to incorporate the Consumer Confidence 
Report Rule Revisions into proposed regulatory text for Regulation 11, achieving widespread agreement 
on a framework that maintained federal stringency while improving comprehension and organization. In 
addition to the Consumer Confidence Report Rule Revisions, the Division proposed minor miscellaneous 
updates throughout Regulation 11 to remove outdated references and increase clarity and correct minor 
discrepancies between federal language in other sections. The Division’s proposed changes were 
straightforward and stakeholders provided no comments on the changes. 
The Commission agrees with the Division that the above revisions will significantly improve both the 
accessibility and clarity of Consumer Confidence Reports, ensuring consumers receive critical information 
in a timely manner through expanded electronic delivery options and biannual reports for larger 
communities. 
Additional Amendments 
The Commission made the following amendments to be consistent with Department practices, to add 
clarity, or update outdated requirements: 
• 
11.1(5)(c) - Addition of reference for suppliers to contact the Division to receive a public water 
system identification number to align with the implementation requirements in Section 11.5 - 
Monitoring Plan Rule. 
• 
11.2(6) - Revision to version date of materials incorporated by reference.

•

•

residual treatment technique requirements for surface water systems. 
• 
11.11(2)(b)(ii), (iii), and (iv) - Removal of outdated requirements for extensions of the minimum 
residual treatment technique requirements for groundwater systems. 
• 
11.16(11)(b)(iii) - Addition of treatment technique violation under Revised Total Coliform Rule for 
failure to have a certified operator. 
•

distribute Tier 1 public notice and to provide copy of the notice to the EPA administrator. 
•

detection limits. 
• 
11.33(7)(a) and (b) - Modification of public notice reporting requirements section to include Tier 1 
Lead Action Level Exceedance requirements under the Lead and Copper Rule Revisions. 
• 
Typographical errors, renumbering, and updated cross references revised as necessary 
throughout Regulation 11.
